jokes.text: If I had a key, I would go in there like every morning and read the whole paper with a coffee or, like, an Oprah chai.
jokes.setup: Abbi is ranting about Gramercy Park being private and inaccessible to them.
jokes.punchline: Her fantasy of rebellion is completely genteel — reading a paper with a branded chai latte. The specificity of 'Oprah chai' undercuts any revolutionary energy.
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy — the joke IS Abbi. She's furious about class gatekeeping but her transgressive fantasy is basically a Starbucks morning routine. The brand specificity ('Oprah chai') is a precise, funny detail that locates her exactly in the aspirational-broke-millennial register.

jokes.text: If I had a key, I'd just grow some fucking weed.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just delivered a cozy, aspirational fantasy about the park. The conversation is in a wistful register.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's idea is immediately practical, criminal, and completely on-brand — she cuts through the reverie with pure Ilana logic.
jokes.explanation: A tight, perfectly economical contrast joke. Abbi's sentiment is soft and aspirational; Ilana's is exactly five words and purely utilitarian-criminal. The joke works because it's structurally a deflation AND a character summary. 'Grow some fucking weed' is the entire Ilana Wexler thesis statement in five words. High character integration score is warranted — only Ilana says this.

jokes.text: Dude, that is genius. Rich people never get caught.
jokes.setup: Ilana floats growing weed in a private park as a scheme.
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately endorses it with a class-conscious rationalization — she's not wrong, but her earnest delivery of a cynical systemic truth as practical advice is the gag.
jokes.explanation: A nice button on the opening exchange. The joke is that it's true — rich people rarely get caught — so the absurdist scheme becomes oddly reasonable. Functional but not extraordinary.

jokes.text: She knows the Heimlich. She knows it now. She took a course!
jokes.setup: A woman is choking on the other side of the gate. Ilana is shouting at her to come to the gate so Abbi can help.
jokes.punchline: The bureaucratic gate prevents them from helping a choking woman. Ilana's escalating credentials pitch ('She knows it NOW. She took a course!') during a life-threatening emergency is absurdly misplaced salesmanship.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is tonal — Ilana is doing an infomercial pitch mid-emergency. 'She knows it NOW' with the implied emphasis captures Ilana's particular brand of chaotic helpfulness. The social absurdity (gate blocking lifesaving help) layers nicely beneath the character comedy.

jokes.text: How can I ever repay you for saving my life?
jokes.setup: They just saved a stranger's life through a gate. She's overwhelmed and grateful, asking how she can ever repay them.
jokes.punchline: The setup implies she'll give them what they wanted — access to the park. But the next beat reveals she refuses entirely.
jokes.explanation: Primarily functions as setup for the stronger punchline that follows. The line itself is a straight feed — the comedy lands in the refusal, not here.

jokes.text: You have to have a key to come in. You have one.
jokes.setup: The woman asks how she can repay them. They diplomatically request a tour of the park. She flatly refuses, citing the key rule.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's deadpan comeback — 'You have one' — after the woman uses the rule as a shield against the people who just saved her life is a perfectly timed skewer of class entitlement.
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly encapsulate the hypocrisy of class gatekeeping. The structure is immaculate: woman invokes an absurd rule, Abbi neutralizes it in one beat. Economy score is near-perfect — 'You have one' does enormous work. The rewatch value is in the beat and delivery. Approaches 8 on craft because of near-perfect economy and structure; the joke doesn't quite reach cultural-touchstone status but is a genuine highlight.

jokes.text: Shrug? Sometimes, I literally hate... humanity.
jokes.setup: The woman has just turned them away after they saved her life.
jokes.punchline: Ilana vocalizes the word 'shrug' rather than just shrugging, then escalates to condemning all of humanity. The gap between the gesture and the conclusion is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Saying 'shrug' aloud instead of doing it is a very specific Gen-Y/internet-voice affectation that Ilana would absolutely do. The jump from shrug to 'I literally hate humanity' is a nice escalation. Character-specific and well-delivered as a button on the cold open sequence.

jokes.text: This rich baby doesn't know how to eat.
jokes.setup: A woman — presumably the park owner — has just choked on her food in a private park.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's editorial commentary reframes the near-death experience as a class-based personal failing. 'Rich baby' is a perfect characterization.
jokes.explanation: The disdain is efficient and funny. 'Doesn't know how to eat' as a critique of the wealthy — after they've just saved her from choking — is a good twist on the class theme running through the cold open.

jokes.text: You are like a Colombian drug lord. That's not gonna work now.
jokes.setup: Ilana has presumably dressed up in something outrageous (inferred from context — she's described as looking like a Colombian drug lord, and the follow-up 'that's not gonna work now' implies she's in an impractical outfit).
jokes.punchline: The compliment is delivered as a straight compliment ('Thank you'), then Abbi immediately undercuts it with practical reality. Calling someone a drug lord as a compliment is pure Ilana-world logic.
jokes.explanation: Classic Broad City compliment structure — the highest praise Ilana could receive in her own register is being likened to a drug lord, and she accepts it graciously. The 'not gonna work now' adds a practical beat that makes the gag land.

jokes.text: I worked seven double-shifts in a row.
jokes.setup: The girls are celebrating Abbi's day off.
jokes.punchline: Seven double shifts is a comically excessive, borderline inhuman number that recontextualizes their celebration as deserved survival rather than leisure.
jokes.explanation: Functional exposition-as-comedy. The number is hyperbolic enough to be funny but it doesn't do much beyond establishing stakes. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: A king for two queens, bitch!
jokes.setup: Abbi is celebrating having fresh cash and a day off, about to reveal what she bought herself.
jokes.punchline: She's bought herself a king-sized bed — a luxury that represents real adult prosperity for her. The phrasing ('two queens') and the 'bitch' button make it an Abbi-does-Ilana delivery.
jokes.explanation: A fun energy burst. The 'two queens' wordplay (playing cards/royalty vs. gay slang) is a minor bonus. Abbi adopting Ilana's vocabulary register to announce a thoroughly Abbi purchase is a nice character irony.

jokes.text: Speaking of orgies, I need to go put on my penis cream.
jokes.setup: Abbi says you can fit four people comfortably in the bed.
jokes.punchline: 'Speaking of orgies' — Ilana's logical leap from 'four people fit in a bed' to orgies, then her immediate pivot to 'penis cream' as a casual domestic task, is a two-beat absurdist pile-on.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one line: the absurd 'speaking of orgies' pivot is funny on its own, but 'penis cream' as a casual household errand is what makes it Ilana-specific and memorable. High character integration — only Ilana treats penis cream like chapstick. Economy is excellent; the line is small but dense.

jokes.text: Do you need any help? Again, I do not think that's appropriate, Ilana.
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she needs to put on penis cream.
jokes.punchline: Abbi reflexively offers to help, then catches herself — 'Again' implies this offer has been made and refused before, which is both a character beat and an implicit callback to prior episodes.
jokes.explanation: The word 'Again' is doing enormous work here — it implies a whole history of Abbi instinctively offering to help with intimate tasks and being gently rebuffed. This deepens the friendship comedy without needing exposition. Rewatch bonus because the more you know the characters, the funnier it is.

jokes.text: I think it's my chest hair. Here. Let me... I got it.
jokes.setup: Abbi tells her new boyfriend Mike that he has something in his teeth.
jokes.punchline: It's his own chest hair. He has been eating so aggressively at his own chest that hair transferred to his teeth. He says this calmly and retrieves it himself.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely inventive gross-out gag. Chest hair in teeth requires an absurd series of events and is specific enough to be disgusting in a funny way rather than just disgusting. The matter-of-fact delivery ('Here. Let me... I got it') elevates it — he's not embarrassed, which makes it worse.

jokes.text: Yeah. Like, on your chest.
jokes.setup: Mike says 'I guess I was really going to town' after retrieving his chest hair from his own teeth.
jokes.punchline: Abbi clarifies — deadpan — 'Like, on your chest.' She's processing in real time and the clarification itself is the joke: she needs to say it out loud to believe it happened.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button. Four words, maximum cringe payoff. The deadpan specificity of 'like, on your chest' is the sound of Abbi's brain refusing to accept the situation. Strong economy score.

jokes.text: Excusez-moi, mademoiselle, but, uh, who da' fresh man meant?
jokes.setup: Abbi walks out and is startled by Bevers, her unwanted roommate, who is apparently in some kind of getup or doing something absurd in her apartment.
jokes.punchline: Bevers is doing a fake French accent while asking who the 'fresh man meant' — almost certainly a mangled reference to 'freshment' or just him being Bevers in full Bevers mode. The joke is Bevers's existence and his commitment to the bit.
jokes.explanation: Bevers is a recurring cringe figure in the show. His function is comic discomfort and misplaced enthusiasm. This joke works mostly on character recognition — first-time viewers may find it confusing, but established fans get the full Bevers experience.

jokes.text: I haven't had my ears candled in a while, but it sounded to me like he gave you multiples.
jokes.setup: Abbi is telling Ilana about her new boyfriend while Ilana was apparently listening to them have sex.
jokes.punchline: Ilana gives a quasi-medical, aurally-caveatted assessment of the sex she overheard. The ear candling caveat is absurd and specific; treating overheard sex as diagnostic data is very Ilana.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent — the ear candling caveat is a completely unnecessary, highly specific medical disclaimer that elevates the joke from 'Ilana listened to Abbi have sex' to something much stranger. The clinical framing ('it sounded to me like') makes it funnier than a straight observation would be. High character integration: this joke could not come from any other character.

jokes.text: Okay, were you listening to us have sex? Yeah. Just listening.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks directly if Ilana was listening to them have sex.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's flat 'Yeah. Just listening.' — the 'just' implying there's a range of more intense options she chose not to engage in — is a great deadpan.
jokes.explanation: The economy here is superb. 'Just listening' implies a whole spectrum of more invasive things Ilana elected not to do, which is scarier than if she'd done them. The confidence of the delivery — she has no shame — makes it funnier. Simple, well-structured, very character-specific.

jokes.text: But I also think that's really about me knowing my body. Yeah, I've noticed that too and I'm proud of you.
jokes.setup: Abbi attributes her good sex to Mike's skills, then pivots to attributing it to her own body knowledge.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'I've noticed that too and I'm proud of you' — responding to Abbi's self-congratulatory body knowledge claim — is strange (how has Ilana noticed?) and weirdly maternal, which is quintessential Ilana-Abbi dynamic comedy.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Ilana's inappropriate familiarity with Abbi's body — 'I've noticed that too' implies ongoing surveillance — but she frames it as maternal pride. The tonal whiplash is very Broad City.

jokes.text: God, it is so moist in this bed.
jokes.setup: Ilana has been lying in Abbi's new bed, where Abbi and Mike have recently had sex.
jokes.punchline: Ilana casually notices and announces the wetness of the post-coital bed with zero social awareness. 'Moist' amplifies the disgust.
jokes.explanation: Simple, effective gross-out. The word 'moist' is doing work. The casualness of the delivery is what makes it land.

jokes.text: I'm Matthew Bevers, I'm Abbi's roommate. Oh, hey, um... Mike, I heard.
jokes.setup: Bevers introduces himself to Mike in Abbi's post-sex bedroom.
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'I heard' — meaning he heard Bevers's name just mentioned, but also implying he's heard Bevers doing various things — is a small, loaded exchange. The scene is inherently cringe: Bevers has barged into a post-sex bedroom.
jokes.explanation: A functional cringe beat. Not the sharpest joke in the sequence but contributes to the Bevers-as-social-nightmare motif.

jokes.text: Abbi's emphatic 'Out!' — presumably accompanied by a pointed gesture ejecting Bevers from the room.
jokes.setup: The entire absurd scene of Bevers and his girlfriend being in Abbi's bedroom post-sex.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's single-word explosion — 'Out!' — as the only possible response to the accumulated weirdness.
jokes.explanation: The comedic release valve after the accumulated Bevers awkwardness. One word carrying all of Abbi's exasperation. The escalation makes the payoff proportionate.

jokes.text: These feet. Very badly abused. 'Ballerina feet.' Just shave the sides off a little and slap on some color. Don't be afraid to really grind with that pumice.
jokes.setup: Abbi is getting a pedicure for the first time without bringing her own nail color — a milestone she announces. The salon worker examines her feet.
jokes.punchline: The worker delivers an unsolicited, clinical, brutal assessment of Abbi's feet — specifically calling them 'ballerina feet' as a euphemism for horrific — with the matter-of-factness of a medical professional.
jokes.explanation: The clinical euphemism ('ballerina feet') followed by the practical solution ('just shave the sides') is an excellent cringe beat. The worker's total detachment makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Nails not much better. Thin, baby nails. Are you working class?
jokes.setup: The worker has already roasted Abbi's feet and moves to her nails.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from physical assessment to direct class interrogation — 'Are you working class?' — as a logical diagnosis for nail quality is both funny and sharp social commentary. The escalation from feet to class status is a great structural move.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely sharp writing. The joke operates on multiple levels: (1) nails as physical evidence of class, (2) the worker's bluntness as professional directness rather than rudeness, (3) it recontextualizes the whole beauty service as a class signifier. The economy is excellent — 'Thin, baby nails. Are you working class?' is only seven words past the setup and contains a complete sociological observation. Approaches 8 on craft; the originality of treating manicure diagnostics as class archaeology is genuinely clever.

jokes.text: I'll have what she's having.
jokes.setup: Ilana observes the brutal treatment Abbi's feet are receiving from the worker.
jokes.punchline: The 'I'll have what she's having' is a When Harry Met Sally reference deployed as a request for the full brutal pedicure treatment — Ilana wants to be roasted and scrubbed just as hard. The meta-reference recontextualizes the nail salon ordeal as desirable.
jokes.explanation: A well-deployed reference that earns its citation by being genuinely subversive — the famous orgasm scene reference is used here to express desire for physical abuse. The inversion is clever.

jokes.text: Money is no object... for me, although, I appreciate a good laugh.
jokes.setup: The worker tells Ilana the full treatment is very expensive.
jokes.punchline: Ilana tries to perform wealthy nonchalance with 'Money is no object,' then immediately qualifies it with 'for me, although, I appreciate a good laugh' — which implies she knows it IS an object and the caveat reveals her actual financial reality.
jokes.explanation: The attempted flex that collapses mid-sentence is a great structure. 'I appreciate a good laugh' as a non-sequitur tag is specifically weird in a way that feels improvised and real. Good character work.

jokes.text: ♪ White guilt, white guilt / White guilt ♪
jokes.setup: Ilana has just made a well-intentioned but catastrophically condescending class-solidarity gesture to the salon owner.
jokes.punchline: Abbi musically narrates Ilana's social failure with a little sung refrain — 'White guilt' — which is both a precise diagnosis and a funny editorial intrusion.
jokes.explanation: The sung commentary works as a meta moment — Abbi steps slightly outside the scene to categorize what just happened. 'White guilt' as a self-aware refrain is both funny and politically sharp. Functions as a button/callback to the owner reveal.

jokes.text: And I realized that a clit looks a lot like a swoosh. And that's how the logo was born. People don't even realize they're walking around with clits all over their shoes.
jokes.setup: A brief, unexplained scene — someone is apparently giving a talk or presentation. We hear them mid-monologue.
jokes.punchline: The claim that the Nike swoosh was designed after a clitoris is delivered with total confidence as historical fact. The tag — 'people don't even realize they're walking around with clits all over their shoes' — elevates it from absurdist factoid to surreal triumph.
jokes.explanation: This is a standout absurdist joke. The confidence with which a completely insane theory is presented as factual history is a classic Broad City move. 'People don't even realize they're walking around with clits all over their shoes' is a genuinely quotable line — the image is vivid and the logic is impeccably deranged. High originality; it's a very specific, novel comedic premise. Character integration scores lower because we don't know whose joke this is.

jokes.text: I'm just, like, that kinda gal, you know? Woman... girl. Woman... you know what? It's not about me.
jokes.setup: Abbi is oversharing about her relationship at work.
jokes.punchline: Her real-time self-correction spiral — 'gal... woman... girl... woman... it's not about me' — while ostensibly talking about something else is a perfect observation about millennial self-policing and awkward self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: This joke is very specifically about the millennial experience of not knowing what to call yourself — 'girl' feels juvenile, 'woman' feels too formal — and it's a recognizable, well-observed moment. The pivot to 'it's not about me' is a nice button.

jokes.text: Do you wanna get back to the clit talk? No, we're... we're kinda past that, Abbi.
jokes.setup: The office meeting has moved past whatever clit-related topic was being discussed.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's attempt to return to 'the clit talk' in a professional setting, and her coworker's weary 'we're kinda past that, Abbi' — delivered as if this happens regularly — is good workplace comedy.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'the clit talk' in an office context is inherently funny. The coworker's resigned response suggests this is par for the course at this workplace. Good tone-setting.

jokes.text: Do you have a harness in this size? / Can you remind me again why you asked me to meet you at what turned out to be a sex store? / Because I need a second opinion on a new leotard.
jokes.setup: Eliot (Ilana's coworker/gay friend) has been summoned to meet Ilana somewhere and is only now realizing where.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Ilana needed a second opinion on a leotard — a completely mundane fashion request — while standing in a sex store is the joke. Her conflation of a sex store with a general clothing shop is very Ilana.
jokes.explanation: The character comedy is solid — Ilana sees no distinction between a sex store and a clothing store, because to her there isn't one. Eliot's framing ('what turned out to be a sex store') is great — he discovered this in real time.

jokes.text: Ilana meets Eliot at what she told him was a 'leotard shop' — it's actually a sex store
jokes.setup: Eliot agreed to help Ilana pick out a new leotard; he arrives to discover the store is a sex shop
jokes.punchline: Eliot: 'Can you remind me again why you asked me to meet you at what turned out to be a sex store?' — the formal phrasing 'what turned out to be' is the joke
jokes.explanation: The setup works because Ilana has pulled the classic 'I need a second opinion on a leotard' bait-and-switch. Eliot's formal indignation is funny — 'what turned out to be a sex store' has the quality of a police report.

jokes.text: Why don't you ask Jaimé? / Gays are not interchangeable, Eliot. Don't be a fucking homophobe. / I'm gay.
jokes.setup: Eliot asks why Ilana didn't ask her gay friend Jaimé to come instead.
jokes.punchline: Ilana accuses him of homophobia for suggesting gays are interchangeable; Eliot points out he IS gay. The structure is a classic: someone calling out discrimination while discriminating against the person they're defending.
jokes.explanation: A tight three-beat joke with a perfect structure: accusation, counter-accusation, reveal. The joke at Ilana's expense — she's calling out homophobia while being homophobic — is well-executed and the pacing is tight. 'I'm gay' as a deflating final beat earns a high impact score.

jokes.text: Okay, fine. Your boobs look... b... bountiful. Or whatever, I don't know. / Delicious. / Thank you.
jokes.setup: Eliot has been bribed with Levain cookies to assess Ilana's appearance in a leotard.
jokes.punchline: His visible discomfort producing 'bountiful' (a deeply hesitant, almost Victorian adjective for breasts), Ilana's helpful upgrade to 'delicious,' and her gracious 'thank you' form a perfect cringe triangle.
jokes.explanation: The stuttered 'b... bountiful' conveys Eliot's discomfort beautifully — it's the word of a man reaching desperately for something safe. Ilana's correction to 'delicious' as an improvement reveals her comfort with her own body. The dynamic between the two characters is well-drawn here.

jokes.text: You wanna come to a sick-ass party tonight? / I don't even know [whose it is]. I just clicked on it on Facebook 'cause I need a freakin' place to flaunt my wealth and my body.
jokes.setup: Ilana invites Eliot to a party.
jokes.punchline: Her stated motivation — 'I need a place to flaunt my wealth and my body' — for attending a random Facebook party is a perfect distillation of Ilana's entire worldview and priorities.
jokes.explanation: 'Flaunt my wealth and my body' is a great Ilana phrase — the juxtaposition of 'wealth' (which she demonstrably doesn't have much of) with 'body' (which she is always confident about) is both funny and revealing. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: No, you hang up first. No, you! No, y... Mike? Hello?
jokes.setup: Abbi is on the phone with Mike doing the 'no you hang up first' bit.
jokes.punchline: He just... hangs up. The bit collapses mid-execution, leaving Abbi saying 'Mike? Hello?' into a dead phone.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the failure of the cutesy relationship ritual. She's doing the coy 'no you hang up' thing and he just... doesn't play along. It's a small foreshadowing of the relationship's actual dynamic. Well-structured as a visual-beat joke.

jokes.text: Did somebody order a hot, rich cunt?
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at the party in her revealing leotard with her new nail tips.
jokes.punchline: Her self-announcement is an entrance line that combines sexual confidence, class aspiration, and profanity into one maximally Ilana greeting.
jokes.explanation: This is deeply character-specific — no other character on television would say this at a party entrance. The word 'cunt' used by a woman to describe herself positively (a reclamation move), paired with the service-industry framing ('did somebody order'), is inspired. High character integration and economy. A quotable, memorable Ilana line.

jokes.text: Ooh, neck hair.
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at the party and presumably sees a man she finds attractive.
jokes.punchline: Her turn-on is specifically neck hair — a very niche, specific detail that perfectly captures Ilana's idiosyncratic sexuality.
jokes.explanation: Two words and a near-perfect character note. Ilana's turn-ons are always specific and surprising. The understatement ('ooh') rather than overt excitement makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You know how I have a gorgeous, uncircumcised penis?
jokes.setup: Jaimé approaches with an urgent look — he has a 'major conundrum.'
jokes.punchline: The conundrum he leads with — as an assumed shared knowledge base — is that he has a 'gorgeous, uncircumcised penis.' The confidence and the implied expectation that everyone at this party knows this is very funny.
jokes.explanation: The setup is the joke — treating his penis as common knowledge that everyone can reference. 'Gorgeous, uncircumcised' as an adjective string is both specific and funny. The whole scene that follows builds from this opener.

jokes.text: Circumcision exposes the penile head decreasing sensitivity over time. The tip becomes calloused, leaving cut penises with, like, five percent of the pleasure experienced by intact men. Pullback gang all damn day. Every day.
jokes.setup: Jaimé is trying to explain why his uncircumcised penis matters for the decision he's considering.
jokes.punchline: He delivers what sounds like a peer-reviewed paper on foreskin sensitivity, then caps it with 'Pullback gang all damn day' — a gang-affiliation-style declaration of foreskin pride.
jokes.explanation: The tonal gap between clinical medical language and 'Pullback gang all damn day' is the joke. The escalation from statistics to gang-affiliation declaration is a great structural choice. 'Pullback gang' is a very quotable, memorable phrase.

jokes.text: Because of my particular PH balance, I am prone... I prone to having the yeasty infections. He has one now. That's why I'm wearing this skirt.
jokes.setup: Jaimé is explaining the conundrum — why he might consider circumcision.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from discussing circumcision philosophically to 'he has one now' — present tense, at a party — and the practical explanation of the skirt is an excellent escalating revelation.
jokes.explanation: Each revelation escalates perfectly: philosophy → medical reality → present tense → visual evidence (the skirt). 'He has one now' as a mid-sentence pivot is excellent economy. The skirt-at-the-party as practical yeast infection management is a genuinely original comedic image. Approaches the vasectomy-escalation archetype from The Office.

jokes.text: I know I 'look good,' but I don't feel sexy.
jokes.setup: Jaimé is explaining his emotional state at the party while managing a yeast infection.
jokes.punchline: The quotes around 'look good' are audible — he's genuinely conflicted about his appearance versus how he feels. The phrase is funny because he clearly DOES look good, and his acknowledgment of it as a separate category from feeling sexy is an unexpectedly nuanced self-assessment.
jokes.explanation: A quieter character moment. The joke is in the self-awareness — Jaimé is both vain and vulnerable. The air-quotes on 'look good' are a nice detail.

jokes.text: That's the one football game I watched. God, you're cute as shit. / Oh, thanks. / You wanna smash later?
jokes.setup: A guy at the party is chatting with Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'You're cute as shit' is already an odd compliment (cute as excrement?), and the pivot from 'you're cute' to 'wanna smash later' with zero transition time is a direct, graceless proposition.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about clumsy male advances. 'Cute as shit' is a slightly funny compliment-insult. The immediacy of 'wanna smash' is the punchline. Competent but not memorable.

jokes.text: Man at party to Abbi: 'You wanna smash later?' Abbi declines. Man: 'Waste of two minutes.'
jokes.setup: A man asks Abbi if she wants to 'smash later' — very direct proposition.
jokes.punchline: When she says no, his disappointed response — 'waste of two minutes' — is both a self-burn (admitting the sex would've been brief) and a sulk.
jokes.explanation: The self-own is what makes this — 'waste of two minutes' inadvertently reveals the sex would have lasted two minutes. The joke attacks the speaker, which makes it the opposite of defensive male posturing. Economy is near-perfect: three words carrying two meanings. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Waste of two minutes.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just turned down the guy's proposition.
jokes.punchline: Her editorial — 'waste of two minutes' — for the entire interaction is a perfect dismissal. The precision of 'two minutes' (not 'a waste of time' generically) is very Abbi.
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum precision. The specificity of 'two minutes' is funnier than a generic response would be. Good economy.

jokes.text: That's what happens when you're taken. Everybody can smell your pheromones spraying off your mons pubis.
jokes.setup: Abbi notes that multiple guys have hit on her tonight.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's scientific explanation involves pheromones and specifically the mons pubis — using clinical anatomical language to describe a completely unscientific folk theory.
jokes.explanation: The pseudo-scientific anatomical specificity ('mons pubis') deployed to explain a nonsensical theory is a signature Ilana move. It's both character-specific and quotable. The image of pheromones 'spraying off' a body part is vivid and gross in a funny way.

jokes.text: It was just the tips. / Ilana, those are dangerous. / Oh, but they do come in handy.
jokes.setup: Ilana's new long nail tips have apparently scratched or hurt someone at the party.
jokes.punchline: 'Come in handy' — a pun on both the idiom and the literal hand/nail context — is a groan-worthy but earned pun, and it sets up the episode's tip/circumcision metaphor theme.
jokes.explanation: The pun is intentional and the episode title is 'Just the Tips' — so this wordplay is load-bearing. Rewatch bonus because the pun connects to the episode's thematic thread about tips/circumcision.

jokes.text: All right, the service in here is terrible. Swear to God, train service and cell service has been terrible since we became a fascist state.
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to get cell service at the party.
jokes.punchline: She escalates from 'bad cell service' directly to 'fascist state' as a causal explanation — a hyperbolic political diagnosis for a mundane inconvenience that is simultaneously a real concern she has.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it's both absurd (cell service → fascism) and earnest — Ilana actually believes this connection. The show was made during a specific political moment and the line reflects it without being preachy. Good character integration.

jokes.text: You know what? I'm gonna work my death drops.
jokes.setup: Ilana can't get cell service; she's trying to find something to do.
jokes.punchline: Her solution to bad cell service is to practice voguing death drops at a party — a completely Ilana solution that combines cultural performance with personal expression.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Ilana doing death drops at a random party is very funny on its own. 'Work my death drops' as a phrase is Ilana at her most expressive. The subsequent physical comedy (presumably her actually doing them) earns a visual gag score.

jokes.text: No, I'm so sorry. I just... I heard you crying. Uh, are you okay?
jokes.setup: Abbi has walked in on a woman crying in what she thought was an unoccupied room.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's apologetic intrusion — 'I was getting service' — into a stranger's private emotional moment sets up a long cringe sequence. The comedy is in the accidental intimacy.
jokes.explanation: More of a scene-setter than a standalone joke. The comedy arrives later in the sequence. This is the awkward premise rather than the punch.

jokes.text: Abbi finds a woman crying in a bathroom at the party. The woman's reason: husband is having marital problems.
jokes.setup: Abbi stumbles on someone having a real emotional moment while trying to find cell service.
jokes.punchline: The scene establishes Abbi — six days into a relationship — as an accidental marriage counselor, setting up the dramatic irony to come.
jokes.explanation: Setup for a longer joke rather than a standalone comedy beat. The comic value is mostly in the structural irony that pays off later. Lower standalone impact.

jokes.text: Abbi walks into a bathroom and discovers someone crying — startled reaction: 'Jesus!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is wandering looking for cell service; encounters something unexpected in a bathroom
jokes.punchline: The 'Jesus!' is the reaction beat — encountering a crying stranger in a party bathroom is both universal and awkward
jokes.explanation: This is primarily a scene setup rather than a joke — it functions to introduce the relationship advice sequence. The 'Jesus!' reaction is functional cringe but not crafted comedy.

jokes.text: Relationships are tough, take it from me.
jokes.setup: A woman is confiding in Abbi about her failing marriage. Abbi presents herself as a relationship authority.
jokes.punchline: The line is funny primarily in retrospect — Abbi has been dating Mike for six days. The dramatic irony is that we know she's not a relationship expert.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in dramatic irony — we know how new this relationship is. The line itself is straightforward, but the context makes it funnier. Rewatch bonus because on second viewing, all of Abbi's relationship advice in this scene is funnier knowing it's six days old.

jokes.text: You look good too. You look like a video girl. / Woman. / Video gi... You look good.
jokes.setup: Lincoln and Ilana are exchanging compliments. He calls her a 'video girl.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana corrects him ('Woman'), he stutters and gives up ('You look good') — a small but precise joke about Lincoln being caught mid-compliment and losing his footing.
jokes.explanation: The gender-term correction mid-compliment — and his inability to recover — is a nice small beat. Connects to Abbi's 'gal/woman/girl' moment earlier in the episode.

jokes.text: Steph, this is Ilana. Ilana, this is my girlfriend, Steph. / Oh... / No... / ♪ Oh, no, oh, no... ♪ / Oh! / Catch up with you later.
jokes.setup: Lincoln introduces Ilana to his girlfriend Steph.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's series of 'Oh...' and 'No...' descending in register as the reality sets in, followed by what appears to be a real-world musical stinger (♪ Oh, no, oh, no ♪) that may be diegetic or Ilana's internal soundtrack, and then her immediate departure — 'Catch up with you later' — is a perfect physical-emotional reaction sequence.
jokes.explanation: The 'Oh... No...' descent is a perfect Ilana reaction — understated at first, then spiraling. The musical cue as emotional punctuation is a visual/sonic joke that Broad City uses well. 'Catch up with you later' as an escape is a great button — she exits with false nonchalance.

jokes.text: Abbi, I just saw Lincoln and then I met his girlfriend and I shit myself from a sphincter-quivering, pelvic-floor-loosening, socially lethal combination of cheese, champagne, and cocaine. I need some working fingers in the bathroom pronto before I Edward Scissors-ass my ass.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just encountered her ex-situationship Lincoln at the party and discovered he has a girlfriend.
jokes.punchline: Her distress manifests as a literally-shit-herself emergency, diagnosed with medical precision ('sphincter-quivering, pelvic-floor-loosening'), attributed to a specific combination of party vices, and culminating in the invented compound word 'Edward Scissors-ass' as a threat of what her nail tips will do to herself during cleanup.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the best jokes in Broad City. Every element is working: the clinical anatomical language ('sphincter-quivering, pelvic-floor-loosening') makes the gross situation funnier by treating it scientifically; 'socially lethal' is the perfect tonal tag ('lethal' implies death, 'socially' miniaturizes it); the three-part cause (cheese, champagne, cocaine) is a perfect comic trio with escalating illegality; 'Edward Scissors-ass' is an invented compound that is immediately comprehensible and horrifying; and 'You owe me a doo doo favor, bitch' is a perfect button. This is Ilana Wexler fully realized — no one else in TV comedy delivers a sentence like this. The structural economy of packing emotional devastation (Lincoln's girlfriend), physical catastrophe (literal defecation), and practical urgency (the nail tips) into one speech is remarkable. Easily justifies 8+ and approaches exceptional.

jokes.text: Hey, this is Abbi. I'm sorry I can't answer the phone, probably with my boyfriend, Mike. We're in a relationship. We're probably... Okay. Thanks for nothing.
jokes.setup: Ilana calls Abbi in the middle of her bathroom emergency. She gets the voicemail.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's voicemail has apparently been updated to mention Mike and their relationship — she recorded it within six days. Ilana's 'Thanks for nothing' to the voicemail is a good reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: The detail of Abbi updating her voicemail to mention Mike within six days is a great character callback — it continues the 'Abbi is over-invested in this relationship' thread. Ilana's delivered insult to a voicemail machine is very funny given the emergency context.

jokes.text: In use! / Ilana, it's me.
jokes.setup: Someone knocks on the bathroom stall Ilana is occupying.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's panicked 'In use!' followed by the recognition of Abbi's voice — 'Ilana, it's me' — sets up the entry.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-transition beat. The comedy lives in what follows.

jokes.text: Oh, hey. / Hey, did you shit your leotard? / No.
jokes.setup: Abbi has entered the bathroom stall where Ilana is having her emergency.
jokes.punchline: 'No' — delivered presumably after a beat, unconvincingly, into a clearly shitty stall — is the joke. The gap between the obvious reality and the flat denial is the humor.
jokes.explanation: The flat 'No' is maximally economical. Its power comes from the context — we know she did, she knows we know, but the denial is automatic. Good deadpan structure.

jokes.text: How'd you know? / I know that face anywhere. That face is saying, 'I'm shitting my leotard.' Also, it really smells like shit in here.
jokes.setup: Ilana has denied shitting herself, then Abbi tells her how she knew.
jokes.punchline: The progression from emotional intelligence ('I know that face anywhere') to physical evidence ('also it really smells like shit') is a perfect Abbi move — she leads with the friendship insight, then adds the obvious.
jokes.explanation: The structural choice to put the emotional insight first and the olfactory evidence second is exactly right — it's funnier because she establishes deep friendship knowledge before adding 'also it smells terrible.' A sweet joke about friendship that happens to be about defecation. 'That face is saying I'm shitting my leotard' is very quotable.

jokes.text: It's just distinct. It smells how it's supposed to smell.
jokes.setup: Abbi observes that the bathroom stall smells bad.
jokes.punchline: Ilana defends the smell as natural and correct — 'it smells how it's supposed to smell' — with the dignity of someone defending a fine wine.
jokes.explanation: 'It smells how it's supposed to smell' is a great piece of Ilana logic — applied to the smell of human waste in a party bathroom, it's both philosophically defensible and absurd. High character integration.

jokes.text: Like I said, I'm open to the idea of circumcision. You know, Damien, I just love the full sensitivity to my penis. An orgasm for me is like a full-body experience — my body fills up with fire and then I just jump into a glacier that's broken up just for me.
jokes.setup: Jaimé is in the bathroom stall with Damien, discussing circumcision while seemingly also helping with Ilana's situation.
jokes.punchline: Jaimé's description of his orgasm as a mystical, elemental experience — fire, then a personalized glacier — delivered with total sincerity is absurdist character comedy at its most committed.
jokes.explanation: The orgasm description is genuinely inventive — fire followed by a personalized glacier is both vivid and insane. 'Broken up just for me' is the detail that makes it — the glacier was customized for his pleasure. This is the kind of absurdist character writing that distinguishes Broad City. High originality score for the imagery.

jokes.text: You know the little basketball hoops that are in the kiddie pool for the kids? Yeah, so I'm dunking on it. Pow! Slam dunk! Wet. And it feels great.
jokes.setup: Jaimé has described his orgasm as fire/glacier. Damien is asked to describe his.
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Jaimé's elemental mysticism and Damien's very specific, physically concrete kiddie basketball metaphor is the joke. 'Wet. And it feels great.' as a coda is delivered with total contentment.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's high points. The contrast joke (mystical vs. kiddie-pool practical) works beautifully. Jaimé's orgasm is cosmic; Damien's is a children's toy. The 'Pow! Slam dunk! Wet.' with the physical gestures implied is hilarious in its specificity and enthusiasm. 'And it feels great.' — delivered with total satisfaction — is the perfect button. Structurally this is excellent contrast comedy: set up the extraordinary, then deliver the mundane with equal enthusiasm. Justifies 8+ on craft.

jokes.text: That's what Mike and I do, and we're great.
jokes.setup: Abbi is dispensing relationship wisdom to a married stranger at a party.
jokes.punchline: Abbi cites her six-day relationship as a model. This works entirely on dramatic irony and is a setup for the 'six days' reveal.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is doing all the heavy lifting. The joke is in what we know that the woman doesn't. Setup piece for the six-days reveal.

jokes.text: Well... it... but our connection is like super powerful. It's like we've known each other for, like, three months, four months.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just revealed it's been six days.
jokes.punchline: Her defense — 'our connection is like super powerful... like three months, four months' — is a beautiful self-own. Three-to-four months is still not much in the context of a marriage.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of her defense — from 'super powerful connection' to 'three, four months' as if that helps — is great. The uncertainty ('three months, four months') while trying to defend a relationship is a funny detail. Good character work.

jokes.text: Where's he from? / From Ohio. / Or is it Iowa? Somewhere...
jokes.setup: The woman is cross-examining Abbi about how well she knows Mike.
jokes.punchline: Abbi doesn't know what state her boyfriend is from. The uncertainty — 'Ohio... or is it Iowa?' — is a great escalating self-revelation.
jokes.explanation: Ohio and Iowa are also funny states to mix up — both middle-America, both vaguely interchangeable to a NYC person. The 'somewhere...' trailing off is perfect.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'It's not that funny.' — as the crying woman continues to laugh hysterically at Abbi's six-day relationship.
jokes.setup: The crying woman is laughing uncontrollably at Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'It's not that funny' — delivered by Abbi as someone who is now the object of ridicule after being the relationship wise-woman.
jokes.explanation: The button on the bathroom sequence. Abbi's wounded dignity is the joke — she knows it is that funny, which is why she has to insist otherwise. Economical and serves the character's humiliation arc well.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. You're not in a relationship, you're literally sleeping with a stranger. You won't even know for, like, years, if you even get that far.
jokes.setup: The stranger whose marriage is falling apart is now coaching Abbi on her relationship.
jokes.punchline: The role reversal — the failing-marriage woman giving spot-on relationship advice to the person who came in to advise her — is a funny and pointed irony.
jokes.explanation: The structural irony (advice-giver becomes advice-receiver) is well-executed. The stranger's clarity about Abbi's situation, while being completely lost in her own, is pointed and funny.

jokes.text: You're such a fucking idiot. / I'm sorry. Oh, my God! / Okay. / Oh, my God. Thank you. I really needed that. / You moron. Six days! / Okay. / It's not that funny.
jokes.setup: The stranger has just realized Abbi's relationship is six days old.
jokes.punchline: Her escalating insults ('fucking idiot' → 'you moron') delivered with apparent catharsis — she needed to call someone an idiot — while Abbi defends herself with 'it's not that funny' is a great role-reversal payoff.
jokes.explanation: The stranger's 'Thank you. I really needed that' after calling Abbi an idiot is genuinely funny — she's redirected her marital frustration productively. 'It's not that funny' from Abbi while clearly it IS that funny to everyone else is a well-timed button. High impact score — this is a crowd-pleaser.

jokes.text: Ilana to Lincoln: 'Steph seems really nice... I hope you guys are making each other orgasm a lot.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln is telling Ilana he's happy in his new relationship.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's well-wish transitions immediately to hoping they 'orgasm a lot' — she expresses goodwill in the only register she knows.
jokes.explanation: The emotional sincerity underneath the joke is what makes it work — Ilana genuinely does wish Lincoln well, and the sexual framing is her authentic love language. It's poignant AND funny, which is the show's best register.

jokes.text: Sorry you had to see me like this. / You're lucky you got out when you did, brother. I am a rich, disgusting monster.
jokes.setup: Lincoln has apparently witnessed the bathroom situation. Ilana apologizes.
jokes.punchline: 'I am a rich, disgusting monster' — said with apparently genuine self-awareness and acceptance — is Ilana's self-assessment delivered at her most vulnerable moment. Calling herself 'rich' in the context of her financial situation is particularly funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Rich, disgusting monster' as self-description is perfectly Ilana — the 'rich' callback to her day-of-spending and the tips, combined with the dignity of owning being 'disgusting' in the current context, makes this a good character moment. 'You're lucky you got out when you did, brother' is a very funny reading of Lincoln's situation.

jokes.text: Yeah, what I just did when you closed your eyes made it worse. Much, much, much worse. It became a medical issue.
jokes.setup: Ilana suggests Lincoln close his eyes while she deals with the situation. He does. She does something. He's told he can open them.
jokes.punchline: Whatever she did — implied to be related to the nail tips and the cleanup — made it 'much, much, much worse' and upgraded the situation from a social problem to 'a medical issue.'
jokes.explanation: The payoff on the 'Edward Scissors-ass' threat — implied to have actually occurred — escalated from bad to medically critical is a great callback payoff. 'It became a medical issue' delivered with the gravity of a diagnosis is the perfect level of understatement for this situation. Rewatch bonus because it pays off the earlier Scissors-ass setup.

jokes.text: There's only one Ilana Wexler.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just confessed to making a bathroom situation medically worse.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's resigned, affectionate, utterly sincere 'There's only one Ilana Wexler' — after witnessing the full Ilana experience — is both a tribute and a thank-god-for-that.
jokes.explanation: One of the most economical lines in the episode. Five words that contain multitudes: affection, relief, exasperation, and a mild alarm. The delivery context makes it land harder — he's saying this while presumably observing the aftermath of a medical bathroom incident. A lovely, earned button on the Lincoln-Ilana storyline.

jokes.text: Someone recently told me that I'm not a relationship person, so I think I just dove into this too fast. I just wanna make sure you know it's me and not you.
jokes.setup: Abbi is breaking up with Mike after six days.
jokes.punchline: Abbi deploys 'it's me not you' — a classic breakup phrase — after a relationship so brief that this phrase has essentially no emotional weight. She's doing the full breakup script for a situation that barely qualifies.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the performance of a full breakup for a six-day relationship. 'It's me not you' is a well-worn phrase but its application here is funny. The character specificity — Abbi performing the rituals of adulthood she's not sure she's ready for — makes it work.

jokes.text: Oh, well, yeah. It's only been six days. I didn't realize we were being exclusive. / What? / Well, I've been sleeping with a couple people.
jokes.setup: Abbi is carefully delivering a rehearsed breakup speech about needing time for herself.
jokes.punchline: He's been sleeping with other people the whole time — she was never in an exclusive relationship in the first place. The earnest breakup speech was entirely one-sided. The deflation is complete.
jokes.explanation: An excellent structural payoff. The joke has a long runway — Abbi has been overinvesting in and performing this relationship for the whole episode — and the reveal that he was never exclusive is the perfect puncture. The structure is nearly perfect: setup across many scenes, payoff in a few words. 'Well, I've been sleeping with a couple people' delivered with zero drama is the ideal punchline tone. Justifies 8+ through structural excellence and the episode-long payoff.

jokes.text: Okay, well, I guess there's nothing more to say unless you have something to say. / I lost the heartbeat!
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to exit the conversation with dignity after finding out she wasn't exclusive.
jokes.punchline: Mike interrupts — not with emotional content — but because he's literally lost a heartbeat (he's a paramedic). The complete mismatch between Abbi's emotional register and his professional urgency is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A great button. The emotional breakup scene is interrupted by a medical emergency — a near-literal version of her being upstaged. The comedy of 'I lost the heartbeat!' in this context is excellent: it could refer to their relationship (metaphorically) but he means it literally. The professional urgency puncturing the romantic moment is structurally very satisfying.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I lost the heartbeat!' — after being told Mike was sleeping with other people.
jokes.setup: Abbi attempts to maintain composure while processing the news that her six-day relationship was not exclusive.
jokes.punchline: 'I lost the heartbeat!' — Abbi using medical language (possibly from her paramedic boyfriend context) for her emotional state, or perhaps literally losing track of what she was doing.
jokes.explanation: The context is slightly unclear in transcript form, but 'I lost the heartbeat' as Abbi's expression of emotional collapse is a good quick beat. The medical metaphor fits with Mike's paramedic job. Works as a brief character reaction.

jokes.text: How'd it go? / I... I let him down easy. Yeah, it was tough. / Good girl. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been the one blindsided in the breakup. Ilana asks how it went.
jokes.punchline: Abbi performs having broken HIS heart ('let him down easy,' 'it was tough') when in fact she was the one devastated. Ilana's 'Good girl' treats this at face value. The self-deception is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The self-protective narrative reversal — she was rejected but performs having done the rejecting — is funny and true. Ilana's 'Good girl' accepts the story without questioning it, which is also a friendship gesture. Good structural irony.

jokes.text: But I can't stuff my tips into my empty heart. / I think it is time for me to lose my tip too. / It's like they're leaving office all over again.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Jaimé are commiserating. Ilana is removing her nail tips; Jaimé is deciding to get circumcised.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's political metaphor — the nail tips 'leaving office all over again' — applied to personal loss is a deranged and funny connection. The parallel between nail-tip removal and circumcision as simultaneous acts of self-reinvention is the episode's thematic joke.
jokes.explanation: The political comparison — nail tips leaving like an administration leaving office — is a funny, specific Ilana move. It connects her personal loss (Lincoln, the evening's events) to the show's political awareness without being heavy-handed. 'I can't stuff my tips into my empty heart' is also a genuinely good line — dramatic and self-aware.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I don't have, um, a like... a tip metaphor for me, personally, for today.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé and Ilana are both using 'tips' as a metaphor for their personal losses/changes; Abbi joins the emotional processing scene.
jokes.punchline: Abbi admits she doesn't have a tip metaphor — breaking the pattern with complete self-awareness about the episode's metaphor structure.
jokes.explanation: Meta-structural joke — Abbi acknowledges the episode's metaphor system she's not part of. The joke is in her self-aware exclusion from the symbolic order ('I don't have a tip metaphor') while still participating in the emotional scene. Formally clever and character-consistent (Abbi is the show's more grounded figure).

jokes.text: Maybe I'm not a relationship girl, you know? Maybe I'm not not one, either. Like, maybe I'm just figuring it all out.
jokes.setup: Abbi is reflecting on the day's events while her nails are being removed.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe I'm not not one, either' — the double negative as emotional hedging — is a perfect Abbi formulation. The joke is in her inability to commit to even a characterization of herself.
jokes.explanation: The double negative construction is a specific, funny linguistic choice — it captures Abbi's commitment-aversion to self-knowledge. The episode ends on this ambivalent note, which is very Broad City: growth, but not too definitively. Good character integration as an episode-closing statement.
